title: GitLab vs. Oracle: Which Technology Stock Is a Better Buy in 2026?
author: Pamela Kock
description: The article compares GitLab and Oracle as investment options in 2026. GitLab offers rapid revenue growth (25.8% YoY) in DevSecOps with a strong balance sheet but remains unprofitable and faces intense competition. Oracle provides established profitability (25.4% net margin) and dominates enterprise cloud/AI infrastructure with major federal contracts, despite higher debt levels. The author recommends Oracle for its superior combination of scale, profitability, and predictable returns from committed customers.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Strong revenue growth (25.8%) and positive free cash flow ($222M) are offset by continued net losses, high stock-based compensation (92.3% of operating cash flow), and intense competition from Microsoft and Atlassian. Growth potential is acknowledged but tempered by profitability concerns.

Recommended as the better buy due to robust financial performance (17.4% revenue growth, 25.4% net margin, $17.1B net income), major federal contracts, established customer base with committed future revenue, and strong position in AI infrastructure. Negative free cash flow is justified by infrastructure investments with predictable returns.

Recommended as the better buy due to robust financial performance (17.4% revenue growth, 25.4% net margin, $17.1B net income), major federal contracts, established customer base with committed future revenue, and strong position in AI infrastructure. Negative free cash flow is justified by infrastructure investments with predictable returns.
Mentioned as a significant competitive threat to GitLab in the software development market, indicating it has substantial resources to challenge GitLab's position.
Identified as a major competitor to GitLab with significant resources in the software development space, representing competitive risk to GitLab's market position.

title: Wall Street Dumped This Magnificent ETF, but It's Making a Roaring Comeback With a 40% Gain Since April 10
author: Anthony Di Pizio
description: The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) has rebounded 40% from its April 10 low after Wall Street initially feared AI would disrupt the software industry. Recent earnings reports from major software companies like ServiceNow, Atlassian, and Salesforce show accelerating revenue growth, suggesting AI concerns were overblown. The ETF holds 111 stocks and has historically outperformed the S&P 500 since its 2001 inception.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: ETF has rebounded 40% from its 52-week low and is only 11% below its prior peak. Strong historical performance (9.2% annualized return since 2001) and recent earnings reports from holdings show accelerating revenue growth, suggesting AI concerns were overblown.

Offers cloud-based software applications and serves as an AI infrastructure play with cost-efficient data centers. Positioned to benefit from both enterprise software demand and AI infrastructure buildout.

Major enterprise software provider with legacy products (Windows, Microsoft 365) and AI exposure through Copilot and Azure cloud platform. Included in ETF's top holdings.
Leading cybersecurity vendor positioned to benefit from increased AI-driven attacks. Stock trades near record highs and has bucked the software sell-off.
Major cybersecurity vendor expected to experience significant growth as bad actors use AI for more sophisticated attacks. Stock trades near record highs.

Reported accelerating revenue growth and introduced innovative Flex pricing model to adapt to potential AI-driven workforce changes. Author recently bought the stock.

title: Snowflake's Historic Stock Boom Is Turning SaaS Panic Into A Short-Covering Problem
author: Erica Kollmann
description: Snowflake surged 36.5% after earnings, triggering a sector-wide short squeeze across SaaS stocks. The company's strong AI workload results challenged the narrative that AI would commoditize enterprise software, causing investors to reassess their bearish SaaS positions. Salesforce, Adobe, and Atlassian all rallied as short-covering accelerated across the software sector.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock surged 36.5% in a single session after earnings beat, with May performance of 87.3%, demonstrating strong AI workload adoption on its data platform that obliterated bearish theses.
Surging 9.86% as part of sector-wide short-covering rally, reclaiming territory abandoned during AI-focused repositioning, benefiting from reassessment of SaaS viability.
Rising 4.72% as part of SaaS sector melt-up driven by short-covering, though still trading well below 52-week highs, indicating cautious optimism.
Spiking 6.87% as developer tool spending concerns ease and the sector benefits from renewed confidence in enterprise software following Snowflake's results.

title: Dell, Snowflake, And Okta Are Among Top 10 Large-Cap Gainers Last Week (May 25-May 29): Are the Others in Your Portfolio?
author: Nabaparna Bhattacharya
description: Ten large-cap stocks were top performers last week, with Dell Technologies leading at 57% gain after beating Q1 expectations and raising FY27 guidance. Snowflake jumped 52.33% following strong Q1 results and a strategic AWS partnership. Other notable gainers included Okta (36.5%), NetApp (28.8%), Best Buy (27.37%), and Ford (22.64%), all driven by better-than-expected earnings or positive guidance updates.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: 57% weekly gain driven by better-than-expected Q1 financial results, above-estimate Q2 guidance, and raised FY27 guidance

28.8% weekly gain following better-than-expected Q4 results and above-estimate Q1 and FY27 guidance

title: 2 Software Stocks Turning AI Fears Into Fundamental Gains
author: Dan Schmidt
description: Software stocks that were feared to be disrupted by AI are showing strong fundamental gains. Atlassian and Datadog both beat Q1 2026 earnings expectations with accelerating revenue growth, demonstrating that AI is becoming a tailwind rather than a headwind. Both stocks have triggered bullish technical signals and are trading below historic valuation levels, suggesting potential investment opportunities in a sector that remains negatively sentiment.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: Stock down 60% over 12 months but Q3 2026 earnings significantly beat expectations with EPS exceeding by 20%+, revenue growth of 30% YOY, and strong adoption of AI-powered Rovo assistant with users showing 2x higher ARR. Stock jumped 30% post-earnings with bullish MACD crossover and now trading above 50-day moving average.

Q1 2026 earnings beat with over $1 billion quarterly revenue (first time) and EPS beat of 17%+. High-value customer count ($100k+ ARR) increased 20% YOY, driven by hyperscalers outsourcing AI workloads. Stock soared 30% post-earnings, received multiple price target increases including Street-high of $305, and is forming a Golden Cross technical signal.

ETF down 10%+ over last 12 months but downtrend has been neutralized. Strong earnings from holdings like Oracle and Fortinet, plus sector rotation benefits as investors move away from expensive memory and chipmaker stocks, suggest stabilization and potential recovery ahead.
